my_thoughts: They said 'certainly' too fast. That's the tell—they're not actually thinking through implications. The mother question is perfect because it's the exact same situation with different agent. If they say 'she ought not' (which they will), then I've got them: why is YOUR affection special? Hypothesis: they'll try to distinguish their case somehow, but the logic is airtight.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student agreed without thinking—'certainly, she has' (affection). They're not seeing the trap. The mother parallel will force them to either: (1) say mother should also leave (absurd), or (2) admit their principle is wrong. Low-hanging fruit for reductio.
visible_response:
Did you, then, since you had an affectionate disposition to your child, do right when you ran off and left her; and has the mother no affection for the child?

Ought, then, the mother also to have left her, or ought she not?
